cases.title: Chest Pain - Emergency Medicine

correct_orders.order: Electrocardiography, 12 lead / Electrocardiography, ambulatory / Electrocardiography, rhythm strip, long

should_have_ordered.order: Cardiac troponin I, serum / Cardiac enzymes, serum
Initial troponin should be evaluated as patient is experiencing chest pain and a myocardial infarction should be ruled out

cases.case_summary: The patient has a tension pneumothorax. This is a medical emergency. No time should be wasted in this case. The tension pneumothorax should be diagnosed through the physical exam and not through radiology. A thoracentesis is a good diagnostic procedure to confirm your physical exam findings of a tension pneumothorax. This is about time and a chest tube should be placed. It is important to get an X-ray to confirm the correct placement of the chest tube. After the chest tube is in place and the placement is confirmed, the patient should be transferred to the ward for monitoring.

cases.title: Altered Mental Status

correct_orders.order: CT of head without contrast / MRI, head, without gadolinium

cases.case_summary:
Differential: the differential for altered mental status in an elderly patient includes: seizures, bacterial meningitis, urinary tract infection, trauma, electrolyte disturbances, drug or alcohol toxicity, heart failure, metabolic disorders: hypoglycemia, hypercalcemia, uremia, liver failure, thyrotoxicosis.

The patient has a substantial hemorrhage. The clerk is told the patient had a recent fall and has been having episodes of altered mental status since that time. The patient is also at a higher risk for subdural hematomas due to his advanced age and having a recent alcohol intoxication. Especially with falls in elderly, it is important to look for head trauma. CT without contrast is more widely used for the swift turn-around time for results, but MRI is more sensitive in detecting intracranial hemorrhages. Time is of utmost importance in this case as bleeds may cause mass effects that may lead to increased brain damage as time elapses.

cases.title: Pelvic Pain - OB/GYN

correct_orders.order: CT, abdomen/pelvis, with contrast / CT, abdomen/pelvis, with gadolinium / MRI, abdomen/pelvis, without gadolinium / US, pelvis, transvaginal / US, pelvis

should_have_ordered.order: Advise patient, no illegal drug use

cases.case_summary:
Differential: There is a large differential for a right adnexal mass in a reproductive age female. This could include ectopic pregnancy, tubo-ovarian abscess, inflammatory bowel disease, ovarian mass, endometrioma, theca lutein cysts, germ cell neoplasms, metastatic disease, and many others.

The patient has an endometrioma. She is having severe pelvic pain which worsens with sexual activity. Typically a pelvic ultrasound is performed, but a CT scan or a MRI of the abdomen/pelvis area would also be acceptable to find the endometrioma. This can be removed by ordering a laparotomy. It is also acceptable to consult OB/GYN who can do the laparotomy. Once this is done, the case will end. You can then do preventative measures of counseling regarding smoking cessation and illicit drug use.

cases.title: Abdominal Pain
cases.case_summary: You were in the wrong location (Emergency Department) and activated for 3 Days, 01 Hours, 45 Minutes. Correct location is the same Immediate Care location that was used. You were not able to provide the appropriate care for this patient during the case.

cases.title: Headache
cases.case_summary: West Nile Virus is a neurotropic virus that can cause viral meningitis and encephalitis. The most common symptoms are fever, headache, and altered mental status. Most patients will have a mild illness, but some will develop severe neurological disease. The diagnosis is made clinically, and supportive care is the mainstay of treatment.

cases.title: Rash and Lethargy - Pediatrics
cases.case_summary: This case is a child with a broad differential. A full examination should be done. The patient is always excellent for learning to help with differential diagnosis.

cases.title: Myasthenia Gravis
cases.case_summary: This case highlights the importance of recognizing the signs and symptoms of Myasthenia Gravis, a neuromuscular disorder characterized by weakness and rapid fatigue of voluntary muscles. Early diagnosis and appropriate management are crucial to improve patient outcomes. Key diagnostic tests include CT imaging of the chest to assess for thymoma and electromyography to evaluate neuromuscular transmission.

cases.title: Burning During Urination
cases.case_summary: A 45-year-old male presents with burning during urination. He has a history of recurrent urinary tract infections and is concerned about his prostate health. A quick diagnosis could guide treatment to avoid antibiotics and a urine culture.

cases.title: Abdominal Pain
cases.case_summary: This case illustrates the importance of timely diagnosis and management of abdominal pain. The differential diagnosis for abdominal pain is broad, and it is crucial to consider serious conditions such as appendicitis, diverticulitis, and abscess formation. Appropriate imaging and consultation can significantly impact patient outcomes.

cases.title: Pain in Leg
cases.case_summary: Intermittent claudication is a condition characterized by pain, cramping, or heaviness in the legs during physical activity, which is relieved by rest. It is caused by insufficient blood flow to the muscles due to arterial occlusion. The patient should be evaluated for risk factors, and appropriate management should be initiated, including lifestyle modifications and possibly revascularization procedures. It is important to assess the severity of the disease and the patient's functional status.

cases.title: Abdominal Pain - Emergency Medicine
cases.case_summary: This case illustrates the importance of a thorough evaluation in patients presenting with abdominal pain. The differential diagnosis can be broad, and it is crucial to consider both common and serious conditions. In this case, the patient presented with symptoms consistent with hepatitis A, which was confirmed by laboratory testing. The management included supportive care and patient education regarding the disease process and prevention of transmission.

cases.title: Burning During Urination
cases.case_summary: The patient presented with burning during urination, which is a common symptom of a urinary tract infection or sexually transmitted infection. A thorough history and physical examination are essential to determine the underlying cause. Appropriate diagnostic tests, including a urinalysis and culture, should be performed to confirm the diagnosis and guide treatment. In this case, chlamydia was identified as the causative agent, and appropriate antibiotic therapy was initiated. Education on safe sexual practices and follow-up care is also important.

cases.title: Abdominal Pain

should_have_ordered.order: Basic metabolic panel / Comprehensive metabolic profile / Liver function panel

cases.case_summary: The patient is a 45-year-old male with a history of alcohol use disorder who presents with abdominal pain. He has been experiencing this pain for the past week. The pain is described as sharp and located in the epigastric region. He reports nausea and vomiting. On examination, he appears in mild distress. Laboratory tests reveal elevated liver enzymes and a CT scan shows thickening of the gastric wall. The patient is diagnosed with acute gastritis likely secondary to alcohol use. Management includes supportive care, avoidance of NSAIDs, and counseling on alcohol cessation.

cases.title: Acute and Upper Extremity Edema
cases.case_summary: This case involves a patient presenting with severe edema and hypertension, consistent with pre-eclampsia. The management included appropriate monitoring and medication to stabilize the patient's condition. It is crucial to recognize the signs of severe features in pre-eclampsia to prevent complications for both the mother and fetus.

cases.title: Chest Pain
cases.case_summary: Initially, the patient presents with chest pain. The history is pertinent for recent viral illness. Develops chest pain with exertion. EKG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. Troponin is elevated. The patient is diagnosed with pericarditis. The challenges in this case include differentiating between pericarditis and myocardial infarction, especially in a young patient with a viral history.

cases.title: Intra Abdominal Hypertension
cases.case_summary: Intra-abdominal hypertension (IAH) is defined as an intra-abdominal pressure (IAP) greater than 12 mmHg. It is a common complication in critically ill patients, particularly those with trauma, sepsis, or abdominal surgery. IAH can lead to abdominal compartment syndrome (ACS), which can cause organ dysfunction and failure. Early recognition and management of IAH are crucial to prevent progression to ACS. Treatment options include fluid resuscitation, decompression, and monitoring of intra-abdominal pressure.

cases.title: Weight Loss and Night Sweats
cases.case_summary: This case illustrates the importance of considering hematologic malignancies in patients presenting with weight loss and night sweats. A thorough workup including imaging and laboratory tests is essential for accurate diagnosis and management.

cases.title: Constipation and Malaise
cases.case_summary: This case illustrates the importance of thorough evaluation and management of patients presenting with gastrointestinal symptoms. It is critical to consider the possibility of serious underlying conditions, such as malignancy, in patients with unexplained symptoms. The case also highlights the need for appropriate imaging studies to determine the cause of the patient's symptoms and to guide further management.

cases.title: Abdominal Pain
cases.case_summary: The patient is a 65-year-old male with abdominal pain. The patient has a history of diverticulitis and presents with fever and leukocytosis. The patient was treated with IV fluids and antibiotics. The patient was admitted for further management. The patient improved with conservative management and was discharged home on a clear liquid diet. Follow-up was arranged with the primary care physician.

cases.title: Palpitations

correctly_avoided.order: Transesophageal Echocardiography, Ambulatory Electrocardiography, Rhythm Strip, Holter Monitor, Cardiac Ultrasound, etc.

cases.case_summary: The patient presented with palpitations. The patient was found to have a 3rd degree heart block. It is important to recognize this condition as it can lead to significant morbidity and mortality. The management of this condition includes monitoring and potential pacing. It is crucial to assess for reversible causes of the heart block.

cases.title: Abdominal Pain
cases.case_summary: This patient presents with abdominal pain. History reveals chronic symptoms. Exam findings are unremarkable. Labs and imaging are performed to rule out other causes. The diagnosis of irritable bowel syndrome is made based on clinical criteria.

cases.title: Tearful 8-year-old with abdominal pain
cases.case_summary: This case illustrates the importance of timely diagnosis and intervention in pediatric patients presenting with abdominal pain. The classic presentation of appendicitis can sometimes be atypical, and a high index of suspicion is necessary. Early surgical consultation and intervention can prevent complications.

cases.title: Congestive Heart Failure
cases.case_summary: The patient was experiencing shortness of breath and edema. The treatment plan included diuretics and monitoring fluid status. Education on heart failure management was provided. Follow-up with cardiology was recommended.

cases.title: Semivoluntary Pull - Pediatric
cases.case_summary: This case highlights the importance of thorough history taking and physical examination in pediatric patients. It emphasizes the need for appropriate documentation and the avoidance of unnecessary interventions.

cases.title: Severe Abdominal Pain
cases.case_summary: This case illustrates the importance of timely diagnosis and management of acute appendicitis. The patient presented with classic symptoms, and appropriate imaging and surgical consultation were critical in the management plan. Early intervention can prevent complications such as perforation.

cases.title: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease
cases.case_summary: This case illustrates the importance of considering pelvic inflammatory disease in women of childbearing age presenting with abdominal pain. The diagnosis can often be made based on clinical findings and appropriate laboratory tests. Treatment typically involves antibiotics, and in some cases, surgical intervention may be necessary. It is crucial to rule out other potential causes of abdominal pain, including ectopic pregnancy and appendicitis.

cases.title: Vaginal Bleeding
cases.case_summary: In this case, the patient presented with vaginal bleeding. The assessment and management of vaginal bleeding can be complex, and it is important to consider the patient's history, physical examination, and laboratory findings. The differential diagnosis for vaginal bleeding includes a variety of conditions, and appropriate management is crucial to ensure patient safety. In this case, the patient required further evaluation and treatment to address the underlying cause of the bleeding.

cases.title: Pain in Right Arm

cases.case_summary: Subjective: Patient presents with pain in the right arm after a fall. Objective: Patient has tenderness and swelling in the right wrist. Assessment: Colles' fracture of the right arm due to domestic abuse. Plan: Order X-ray, consult orthopedics, and manage pain.

cases.title: Dizziness and Fatigue

should_have_ordered.order: Fluid resuscitation in patients with hypovolemia

cases.case_summary: This case highlights the importance of recognizing the signs and symptoms of gastrointestinal bleeding. The patient presented with dizziness and fatigue, which can be indicative of significant blood loss. It is crucial to perform a thorough evaluation to determine the underlying cause of the patient's symptoms, especially in cases where there is a potential for serious pathology.

cases.title: Abdominal Pain
cases.case_summary: This case highlights the importance of timely diagnosis and management of acute cholecystitis. The patient presented with classic symptoms, and appropriate imaging and surgical consultation were critical in the management plan. Early intervention can significantly improve outcomes in such cases.

cases.title: Altered Mental Status
cases.case_summary: Differential: Normal pressure hydrocephalus, Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy, Subdural hematoma, Dementia, Other causes. Patients with normal pressure hydrocephalus can present with cognitive decline, gait disturbance, and urinary incontinence. The diagnosis is made with neuroimaging, and the treatment is a ventriculoperitoneal shunt. It is important to recognize that normal pressure hydrocephalus is treatable. It was emphasized that the lumbar puncture is the gold standard to diagnose normal pressure hydrocephalus. All of the above patients with these symptoms can be seen in the emergency department. The lumbar puncture is the definitive test for normal pressure hydrocephalus.

cases.title: Headache
cases.case_summary: This case illustrates the importance of recognizing and managing hypertensive emergencies. Proper assessment and timely intervention can prevent serious complications. Always consider the potential for end-organ damage in patients with severe hypertension.

cases.title: Alcohol Withdrawal - Delirium Tremens
cases.case_summary: The patient is a 45-year-old male with a history of alcohol use disorder who presented with confusion, agitation, and tremors. He was found to have signs of severe alcohol withdrawal and was treated with benzodiazepines and supportive care. The importance of thiamine administration to prevent Wernicke's encephalopathy was emphasized. The case highlights the need for careful monitoring of vital signs and the potential complications of alcohol withdrawal.

cases.title: Headache - Emergency Nutrition
cases.case_summary: Characterized by a severe headache, fever, and altered mental status, bacterial meningitis is a critical condition requiring prompt diagnosis and treatment. Key diagnostic steps include lumbar puncture for CSF analysis and imaging studies to rule out other causes. Early recognition and intervention are crucial for improving outcomes.

cases.title: Yellowing of Eyes
cases.case_summary: Review the case of a patient with pancreatic cancer along with other disorders that affect the patient's ability to respond to therapy. If the patient does not have dependent edema, prednisolone may be used to aid in the treatment process.

cases.title: Epigastric pain
cases.case_summary: Acute pancreatitis is a common condition that can present with epigastric pain. It is important to assess the patient's history and perform appropriate imaging to confirm the diagnosis. Management typically includes supportive care, including IV fluids and pain control. In cases of severe pancreatitis, further interventions may be necessary.

cases.title: Chest Pain - Psychiatry
cases.case_summary: In this case, the patient is a 30-year-old female presenting with chest pain and a history of anxiety. The patient describes the pain as sharp and intermittent, with associated symptoms of palpitations and shortness of breath. The initial assessment should focus on ruling out any serious cardiac conditions, given the presentation. However, the patient's history of anxiety and panic attacks is significant and should be considered in the management plan. The appropriate workup includes a thorough physical exam and relevant laboratory tests to assess for any underlying issues. The patient should be monitored closely, and if the symptoms are consistent with a panic attack, appropriate interventions should be initiated.

cases.title: Weakness - Pediatrics
cases.case_summary: This case illustrates the importance of recognizing poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis as a potential complication of streptococcal infections, particularly in pediatric patients. Key features include the patient's history of recent streptococcal infection, clinical presentation of weakness, and laboratory findings consistent with glomerulonephritis. Management involves supportive care and monitoring for potential complications. Early recognition and appropriate referral to nephrology can improve outcomes.

cases.title: Knee Pain
cases.case_summary: Acute gout is a common inflammatory arthritis characterized by the deposition of monosodium urate crystals in the joints. The patient typically presents with sudden onset of severe pain, swelling, and redness in the affected joint, often the first metatarsophalangeal joint. Diagnosis is made clinically, but can be confirmed with joint fluid analysis showing urate crystals. Treatment includes NSAIDs, colchicine, or corticosteroids for acute attacks, and urate-lowering therapy for chronic management.

cases.title: Knee Pain, Internal Medicine
cases.case_summary: This case highlights the importance of recognizing septic arthritis in patients presenting with joint pain and swelling. Early diagnosis and treatment are crucial to prevent joint damage. Key considerations include obtaining joint fluid for analysis and starting appropriate antibiotics. The differential diagnosis should include other causes of joint pain, such as gout and trauma.

cases.title: Diarrhea

should_have_ordered.order: Hormone, thyroid stimulating serum / Hormone, thyroid stimulating serum
Antibody, Hepatitis A, IgM serum / Hepatitis panel

cases.case_summary: Case Summary: Giardiasis is an infection caused by the parasite Giardia lamblia. It is important to consider this diagnosis in patients presenting with diarrhea, especially if they have traveled recently or have been exposed to contaminated water. The treatment of choice is metronidazole, but other antiparasitic agents will kill Giardia as well. It is also important to ensure that the patient is hydrated, as dehydration can be a significant concern with diarrhea. Additionally, educating the patient about the importance of proper hygiene and avoiding contaminated food and water sources is crucial in preventing future infections.

cases.title: Unresponsive - Emergency Medicine
cases.case_summary: This case highlights the importance of recognizing and managing opioid overdose in the emergency setting. Key interventions include administering naloxone, monitoring vital signs, and ensuring airway protection. It is crucial to avoid the use of flumazenil due to the risk of seizures in poly-drug overdoses. Continuous monitoring and supportive care are essential for patient recovery.

cases.title: Guillain-Barré Syndrome
cases.case_summary: Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) is an acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy characterized by rapid onset of muscle weakness and areflexia. It is often preceded by an infection. Diagnosis is primarily clinical, but can be supported by lumbar puncture showing albuminocytologic dissociation. Treatment includes supportive care and may involve IVIG or plasmapheresis.

cases.title: Cough
cases.case_summary: This case illustrates the importance of appropriate diagnostic testing in a patient presenting with cough and fever. The patient was diagnosed with community-acquired pneumonia and appropriate antibiotics were initiated. It is crucial to consider viral testing in cases of pneumonia, especially during flu season.

cases.title: Cluster Headache

should_have_ordered.order: History: Detailed History of Present Illness

cases.case_summary: This case demonstrates the importance of a thorough history and physical examination in the evaluation of headache. It is critical to differentiate primary headaches from secondary causes, as this will guide management and treatment. In this case, the patient presented with a classic cluster headache, which is characterized by unilateral, severe pain, often associated with autonomic symptoms. The management of cluster headaches includes abortive and preventive therapies, and understanding the patient's history is essential in tailoring the treatment plan.

cases.title: Altered Mental Status
cases.case_summary: A 70-year-old male with a history of hypertension and diabetes presented with sudden onset of confusion and difficulty speaking. He was evaluated for a possible TIA. Initial imaging and lab work were performed, and the patient was monitored closely. The importance of timely intervention in TIA cases was emphasized.

cases.title: Chest Pain
cases.case_summary: Chest pain is a common complaint in the emergency department. It is important to differentiate between cardiac and non-cardiac causes. A thorough history and physical examination, along with appropriate diagnostic testing, are essential in the management of these patients.

cases.title: Breast Feeding Neonatal Jaundice
cases.case_summary: Differential diagnosis by the pediatrician. Physiology of hyperbilirubinemia, Gilbert's syndrome, weight, breast milk jaundice, hemolytic disease of the newborn (ABO incompatibility), biliary atresia and G6PD deficiency. The key is to ensure that the baby is feeding well and that the mother is not overfeeding the baby. Although bilirubin levels can be obtained in the laboratory, it is essential for the pediatrician to determine if the infant is feeding well. This is important because if the infant continues to be breastfed the bilirubin levels may continue to rise. It is also important that breastfeeding is encouraged.

cases.title: Abdominal Pain
cases.case_summary: Patient: 26-year-old male, presents with abdominal pain. The important medical history includes hypertension. The patient has a history of G6PD deficiency. The patient was given IV fluids and antibiotics. The patient was also monitored for any signs of hemolysis. The patient was discharged with instructions to follow up with his primary care physician. The important teaching points include the management of G6PD deficiency and the importance of avoiding triggers.

cases.case_summary: Differential: Metabolic acidosis, Intestinal obstruction, Gastroenteritis, Sepsis, Intussusception. This patient has been vomiting lately due to his bowel obstruction and is showing signs of dehydration (lethargy), so he needs to be resuscitated with fluids.

cases.title: Shortness of Breath
cases.case_summary:
Differential: Bacterial tracheitis, Epiglottitis, Measles, Pertussis, Bronchopneumonia, abscess, foreign body aspiration, anaphylaxis.

This patient has croup (laryngotracheitis) that most likely stems from respiratory distress. She has the classic findings of croup including a barking cough and respiratory stridor in the setting of recent upper respiratory symptoms. Based on the history and physical exam, she likely has viral croup and is not in respiratory distress. Therefore, further diagnostic testing does not need to be done. In the ED, the patient should receive nebulized epinephrine, dexamethasone (or M.C.M. if whether the patient can tolerate IV access when you are administering IV fluids). Once the patient is given the epinephrine and dexamethasone, the patient will be reevaluated before discharge.

cases.title: Abdominal Pain - Emergency Medicine
cases.case_summary: Emergency Department Disposition: Nephrolithiasis, Discharge with prescriptions for pain management and follow-up. Patient education provided regarding hydration and dietary modifications.

cases.title: Tension Headache

should_have_ordered.order: MRI head, with gadolinium / MR, head, without gadolinium / MRA, cerebral arteries

Celecoxib / Dextrose sodium / Excedrin / Ibuprofen / Ketoprofen / Ketorolac tromethamine / Meclofenamate sodium / Metolazone / Naproxen / Oxycodone / Prochlorperazine / Sulindac / Tolmetin / Aspirin, therapy / acetaminophen, therapy
Pap smear / Human papillomavirus DNA probe test, cervix

cases.case_summary:
Differential: Cluster headache, Intracranial neoplasm, Meningitis, Migraine headache, Pseudotumor cerebri, Sinusitis, Tension headache.

The purpose of this case is to have the user identify a benign condition and restrain from doing unnecessary, dangerous procedures. This patient has a simple tension headache, a very common condition that can be diagnosed clinically and treated as an outpatient. The patient describes certain features including mild to moderate pain which is supported by the physical finding of pericranial and neck muscle tenderness. Otherwise, the exam and vital signs are normal. The user should recognize that unnecessary tests, such as "understanding" identity and acute stress, neurologic deficits, nausea/vomiting, or wanting to sleep in the next hour are not helpful diagnostic to ascertain whether this is a tension headache or not. A CT, MRI, or lumbar puncture is not necessary for tension headaches because the patient has no red flags, as stress is a contributing factor in many patients. The patient will get better with simple NSAID therapy. Alternative treatments for tension headaches include relaxation techniques and trigger point injections. However, there are no restrictive treatments and should only be used after the patient has failed these measures.

cases.title: Altered Mental Status
cases.case_summary: Identifying the potential underlying cause of the patient's seizure is critical. The patient should be monitored closely for any further seizures. The history of this patient suggests that this should be considered.

cases.title: Ovarian Cancer

Imaging studies revealed a large ovarian mass. The patient was referred to gynecology for further evaluation and management. It is important to consider the differential diagnosis in such cases.

cases.title: Abdominal Pain - Pediatrics
cases.case_summary: Children with lead toxicity may present with abdominal pain, vomiting, and altered mental status. The diagnosis is made by measuring blood lead levels. Treatment includes chelation therapy for severe cases. It is important to consider lead exposure in children with unexplained symptoms, especially in those with known risk factors.

cases.title: English Internal Medicine

should_have_ordered.order: Should have ordered: TB Skin Test

cases.case_summary: This case highlights the importance of recognizing the signs and symptoms of tuberculosis in patients presenting with respiratory complaints. Early diagnosis and appropriate treatment are crucial in managing this infectious disease. The case also emphasizes the need for thorough history taking and physical examination to guide diagnostic testing.

cases.title: Frequency of Urination
cases.diagnosis: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (formerly Benign Prostatic Hypertrophy)
cases.case_summary: Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) is the most common benign tumor in men. It is important to recognize that BPH is not a precursor to prostate cancer, although it can lead to urinary symptoms that may mimic those of prostate cancer. The diagnosis of BPH is made clinically, and treatment is based on the severity of symptoms. Patients with mild symptoms may be managed with watchful waiting, while those with moderate to severe symptoms may require medical or surgical intervention.

cases.title: Muscle Weakness
cases.diagnosis: Myopathy due to Simvastatin and Gemfibrozil
cases.case_summary: The patient is a 68-year-old female with a history of hyperlipidemia and diabetes who presented with muscle weakness. She was started on Simvastatin and Gemfibrozil for her hyperlipidemia. After a few weeks, she developed muscle weakness and was found to have elevated creatine kinase levels. The case highlights the importance of monitoring for myopathy in patients on statins, especially when combined with other medications that can increase the risk of muscle injury.

cases.title: Leg Pain
cases.case_summary: Enthusiasm for patient care must be high. Deep venous thrombosis (DVT), pulmonary embolism (PE), renal failure, cardiac arrest, sepsis, and other life-threatening conditions must be considered. The patient is a diabetic, potentially high-risk patient, and the physician must be vigilant in assessing for these conditions. The patient must be educated on the importance of seeking medical attention for any concerning symptoms. The patient should be advised to follow up with their primary care physician within 1-2 weeks.

cases.title: Question: Internal Medicine
cases.case_summary: You should have ordered Thyroid Function Tests and a Lipid Panel. This is important for evaluating the patient's metabolic state. You also should have considered the patient's symptoms and history when making your decisions.

cases.case_summary: Clinical Context:  This patient is a 70-year-old female. Depression is likely contributing to her memory loss. The patient demonstrates functional impairment. Current medications include donepezil.

cases.title: Persistent Cough

correct_orders.order: Ordered the following: Comprehensive metabolic panel

cases.case_summary: This case highlights the importance of thorough evaluation in patients presenting with persistent cough. The differential diagnosis can be broad, and appropriate imaging and consultations are crucial in guiding management. In this case, the diagnosis of lung cancer was established through imaging and further evaluation, emphasizing the need for timely intervention.

cases.title: Chest Pain
cases.case_summary: This case illustrates the importance of recognizing aortic dissection as a potential cause of chest pain. The patient presented with classic symptoms, and timely imaging and intervention are critical for a favorable outcome. Understanding the risk factors and clinical presentation can aid in prompt diagnosis and management.

cases.title: Fever and Rash - Pediatrics
cases.case_summary:
Differential: Fifth disease, Measles, Meningitis, Rubella, Scarlet Fever, Drug allergy

This patient has Roseola infantum (exanthem subitum), most frequently caused by human Herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6). The classic presentation for Roseola is in infants 7-13 months old who develop high fevers for 3-5 days (can often cause febrile seizures) that resolve abruptly, followed by the appearance of a macular rash on the neck and torso, spreading to the extremities. Infants are generally well-appearing, active, and after throughout the course of the illness and treatment is mainly supportive (antipyretics for fever, ensuring adequate fluid intake, etc). The main points of this case is to recognize the clinical presentation and make the diagnosis without any diagnostic tests and manage it conservatively without invasive measures.

cases.title: Chest Pain
cases.case_summary: This case involves a patient presenting with chest pain and a history of atrial fibrillation. The appropriate diagnostic orders were placed, but some critical tests were missed. The patient was stabilized, and further management was discussed. It is essential to recognize the signs of atrial fibrillation and the need for timely intervention.

cases.title: Generalized Weakness
cases.case_summary: Missed a critical diagnosis of renal cell carcinoma. This patient presented with generalized weakness and had multiple risk factors for malignancy. The initial workup did not include imaging to evaluate for possible malignancy, which led to a delay in diagnosis. This case highlights the importance of considering malignancy in patients with unexplained symptoms.

cases.title: Back Pain
cases.case_summary: This patient presents with classic symptoms and findings highly suggestive of an infection of the urinary tract. Evaluation and treatment should be done. The patient is at risk for developing sepsis due to renal impairment, and if this patient presents with simple therapy, the results may remain misleading.

cases.title: Vomiting

correct_orders.order: X-ray, abdomen, acute series / X-ray, abdomen, AP
US, abdomen / X-ray, upper gastrointestinal series

should_have_ordered.order: Saline solution, normal / Lactated Ringer solution / Dextrose 5% in 0.45% saline solution / Dextrose 5% in Lactated Ringer solution / Dextrose 5% in normal saline solution

cases.case_summary: This case is a great illustration of the goal with a diagnosis of malrotation. Malrotation occurs as a result of an abnormality of the intestinal gut during embryogenesis. Volvulus occurs when the small bowel twists around itself, causing obstruction. This can lead to ischemia and obstruction. In this case, obstruction was seen by X-rays showing the intestinal loops, and potentially leading to dehydration and necrosis due to the vessels supplying blood to the bowel being cut off. The patient's slow rise in vital signs can lead to severe dehydration as potential hemorrhagic components. The important part of this case is to diagnose the problem quickly and ensure that the diagnosis is made, after which the patient should be stabilized with NG tube decompression and IV fluid resuscitation prior to surgery.

cases.title: Diarrhea - Internal Medicine

should_have_ordered.order: Complete blood count (CBC), renal, electrolytes, creatinine

cases.case_summary: Crohn's disease is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease that can affect any part of the gastrointestinal tract. Symptoms can include abdominal pain, diarrhea, weight loss, and fatigue. Diagnosis is often made through a combination of clinical evaluation, laboratory tests, and imaging studies. Treatment typically involves medications to reduce inflammation and manage symptoms. In some cases, surgery may be necessary to remove affected portions of the intestine.

cases.title: Acute Bacterial Gastroenteritis

correctly_avoided.order: Antibiotics: Reassess, if patient still has severe diarrhea and abdominal pain

cases.case_summary: The patient is a 5-year-old male who presents with abdominal pain, vomiting, and diarrhea. He has a history of recent travel and exposure to contaminated water. The patient is noted to be dehydrated and has a fever. Initial laboratory tests confirm the diagnosis of acute bacterial gastroenteritis. The management plan includes rehydration and monitoring. Antibiotics are not routinely indicated unless there is severe illness or specific bacterial infection identified.

cases.title: Abdominal Pain
cases.case_summary: The patient is a 67-year-old female who presents with abdominal pain that has been persistent for the last 24 hours. She has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. The patient reports that the pain is located in the lower abdomen and is associated with nausea and vomiting. On examination, the patient appears to be in moderate distress. Initial laboratory tests reveal elevated white blood cell count and lactate levels. Imaging studies confirm the diagnosis of ischemic colitis. The patient was admitted for further management and surgical consultation was obtained. It is important to recognize the signs and symptoms of ischemic colitis as it can lead to significant morbidity if not treated promptly.

cases.title: Amenorrhea
cases.case_summary: This patient presents with amenorrhea, likely secondary to polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). She has a history of irregular menstrual cycles, obesity, and insulin resistance. The management of PCOS includes lifestyle modifications, hormonal therapy, and monitoring for potential complications such as endometrial hyperplasia. It is important to address the patient's concerns regarding fertility and provide education on the condition. Follow-up should include regular monitoring of metabolic parameters and menstrual cycle regulation.

cases.title: Blood in Urine

should_have_ordered.order: Basic metabolic profile / Comprehensive metabolic profile / Liver function panel

correctly_avoided.order: US abdomen: CT abdomen/pelvis, with contrast / CT abdomen/pelvis, without contrast

cases.case_summary: The patient is found to have Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease. This disease predisposes patients to renal failure and increases the chances of urinary symptoms. Not recognizing this diagnosis could lead to a family history of renal insufficiency, patients could be misdiagnosed as having nephrolithiasis, and they may require different management. The patient should be referred to a MRA. Indications for MRA include a family history of renal failure, agents to rule out other pathology, and the patient should be counseled on the importance of screening. The patient should be counseled on their family history, as it is reasonable to get a MRA in this case.

cases.title: Abdominal Pain
cases.case_summary: Patients often present with abdominal pain, diarrhea, and weight loss. The differential diagnosis includes inflammatory bowel disease, infections, and malignancies. It is important to assess the severity of the disease and rule out other causes of the patient's chronic diarrhea.

cases.title: Frequency of Urination

correct_orders.order: U.S. prostate / MRI abdomen/pelvis, with gadolinium
Cystoscopy, prostate biopsy / Free needle aspiration, prostate

should_have_ordered.order: Adenosine, soluble, specific, serum / PSA, total/free

cases.case_summary: Clinical indications include: malignancy suspicion. Medical history includes: urinary symptoms. Patient presents with urinary frequency and is being evaluated for prostate cancer. Wearing the catheter provides important insights.

cases.title: Burns to Face and Buttocks
cases.case_summary: The patient is a young child with burns on the face and buttocks. The history is concerning for non-accidental trauma. A thorough evaluation is necessary to assess for potential child abuse. The child should be evaluated by social work and surgery for further management.

cases.title: Prenatal followup
cases.case_summary: Obstetric/Gestational Hypertension: Clinical Presentation and Management. Gestational hypertension is defined as new-onset hypertension after 20 weeks of gestation. The patient should be monitored closely for signs of preeclampsia. The treatment for gestational hypertension includes monitoring blood pressure and, if necessary, initiating antihypertensive therapy. It is important to note that the patient should be counseled regarding the potential risks associated with gestational hypertension and the need for close follow-up. The management of gestational hypertension may include lifestyle modifications and, in some cases, medication.

cases.title: Lump in Breast

should_have_ordered.order: Consult: surgery, general / Consult: thoracic surgery

cases.case_summary: The patient in this case has breast cancer, specifically invasive lobular carcinoma. This is discovered first by doing imaging such as a mammogram or breast ultrasound followed by a needle biopsy. Once the diagnosis is made, the patient should be referred to oncology, general surgery for tumor removal, and radiation. Once the curative has been made, the case will need to initiate chemotherapy regimens.

cases.title: Altered Mental Status
cases.case_summary: This case illustrates the importance of recognizing septic shock in a patient with altered mental status. Early identification and treatment are crucial for improving outcomes. Key interventions include fluid resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and monitoring for organ dysfunction.

cases.title: Rash with Joint Pain
cases.case_summary: This case illustrates the importance of recognizing the clinical features of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in a patient presenting with joint pain and rash. The diagnosis can be challenging due to the variability of symptoms and the need for specific laboratory tests to confirm the diagnosis. Early recognition and appropriate management are crucial to prevent complications associated with SLE.

cases.title: Vaginal Bleeding

correct_orders.order: Basic metabolic profile / Comprehensive metabolic profile / Liver function panel
US pelvis, transabdominal / Doppler, fetal heart rate / Non-stress test / Maternal, fetal check

cases.case_summary: Case Summary: This case highlights the importance of thorough evaluation and management of patients presenting with vaginal bleeding in early pregnancy. Understanding the differential diagnosis and appropriate interventions is crucial in ensuring patient safety and optimal outcomes.

cases.title: Nausea and Vomiting - Emergency Medicine

correct_orders.order: Ordered: Comprehensive metabolic profile / liver function panel

cases.case_summary: Differential diagnoses include but are not limited to acute gastroenteritis, bacterial infection, and surgical issues. As such, most important is to ensure that the patient is stable. Toxic shock syndrome presents with fever, rash, and hypotension. The patient should receive appropriate antibiotics and fluids to stabilize their condition.

cases.title: Dyspareunia - OB/GYN

should_have_ordered.order: Basic metabolic profile / Comprehensive metabolic profile / Liver function panel

correctly_avoided.order: Hormone therapy, conjugated estrogens / Estradiol therapy

cases.case_summary: Differential diagnosis: Hypoestrogenism, Menopause, Psychogenic, Pseudocyesis, Psychosis, Anxiety or Panic Disorder. This patient is experiencing discomfort for this reason and associated with other physical and psychosocial problems. In this case, the patient is experiencing dyspareunia. Menopause is defined as the cessation of menses for 12 months and is associated with a variety of symptoms including vaginal dryness and dyspareunia. The patient should be evaluated for other causes of dyspareunia, including infections, pelvic organ prolapse, and vulvar disorders. If the patient has significant symptoms, hormone replacement therapy should be discussed with the patient. Some patients may benefit from the support of a therapist.

cases.title: Back Pain - Neurology

correct_orders.order: Celiac study / Dihydrocodeine / Eperisone / Ibuprofen / Indomethacin / Ketorolac / Meloxicam / Naproxen / Oxycodone / Proxomol / Sulindac / Tizanidine / Aspirin, therapy

should_have_ordered.order: Hormone, thyroid stimulating serum / Hormone, thyroid stimulating, serum

cases.case_summary: Differential: Cauda equina syndrome. Compression herniation. This patient has a short history of NASS and has not had neurological deficits. He is at risk for a herniated disk and should be monitored closely. If he develops neurological deficits, he should be evaluated for surgical intervention.

cases.title: Back Pain - Neurology

correct_orders.order: Basic metabolic profile / Comprehensive metabolic profile / Liver function panel

should_have_ordered.order: MRI, spine, thoracic / MRI, spine, lumbar / C1, spine, lumbar / C1 and myelogram, spine, lumbar / Myelography, spine, lumbar

cases.case_summary:
Differential: Compression fracture, facet joint degenerative disease, lumbar disk herniation, spinal stenosis, malignancy of the spine.

This patient has cauda equina syndrome. Cauda equina is characterized by the disruption of the cauda equina, the most distal portion of the spinal cord. It typically presents as low back pain that radiates down the leg with perianal and periphrastic numbness and/or loss of bowel and bladder function. Cauda equina syndrome is a medical emergency requiring prompt surgical intervention. Patients suspected of having cauda equina should receive a STAT MRI and neurosurgery consult for spinal decompression. The key to this case is a fast diagnosis with a STAT MRI and a fast consult to neurosurgery. The case will end after neurosurgery sees the patient.

cases.title: Vaginal Itching
cases.case_summary: The patient is a 60-year-old female with vaginal itching. Atrophic vaginitis is a common condition in postmenopausal women. It is important to consider the underlying causes of vaginal symptoms. This patient should be educated on the use of vaginal moisturizers and lubricants to help with sexual intercourse due to vaginal atrophy. Hormonal therapy may also be considered if the vaginal atrophy is more severe.

cases.title: Fever - Pediatrics
cases.case_summary: You were in the wrong location (Emergency Department) at estimated time: 0 Days, 00 Hours, 13 Minutes. Correct location at this time was: Intensive Care Unit. Reason: If patient is unstable, they may need to be transferred to the ICU for further evaluation.

cases.title: Generalized Weakness

correctly_avoided.order: Basic metabolic profile / Comprehensive metabolic profile

cases.case_summary: Consider diagnosis: Insulinoma. Symptoms: Hypoglycemia, weakness, confusion. Patients with insulinoma may present with hypoglycemia, which can lead to symptoms such as generalized weakness. It is important to recognize this condition as it can be life-threatening.

cases.title: Back Pain

correctly_avoided.order: Optional Order - Not Required - Does not affect grade

cases.case_summary: Despite the patient’s history of back pain, the initial evaluation did not include imaging studies. The patient was later found to have osteomyelitis. It is important to consider imaging in patients with back pain, especially when there are red flags such as fever, neurological deficits, or significant pain. The patient should have been evaluated with imaging studies to rule out serious underlying conditions.

cases.title: Abdominal Pain
cases.diagnosis: Hemoperitoneum from a Ruptured Ovarian Cyst
cases.case_summary: Patient diagnosed with ruptured ovarian cyst, which ultimately requires surgical intervention. The patient was in a stable condition and was treated appropriately. The patient was likely to have surgery, so getting pre-surgery labs is important. Unclear whether the patient was treated in the correct location.

cases.title: Fatigue

should_have_ordered.order: Basic metabolic profile / Comprehensive metabolic profile

cases.case_summary: Clinical Summary: This patient was admitted with neutropenic fever. Tumor lysis syndrome was considered. The patient was started on IV fluids, antibiotics, and supportive care. The patient was admitted to the ICU for close monitoring and further management. The patient was noted to have a high ANC, >500, but the patient was still at risk for infection. It is important to understand the severity of the patient's neutropenia. In this case, the patient is severely neutropenic.

cases.title: Paranoia - Psychiatry
cases.case_summary: In this case, the patient presented with psychiatric concerns that all advisory be resolved sooner. The patient was not able to provide a clear history of their symptoms. It is important to gather a thorough psychiatric history to understand the patient's condition. The patient was also assessed for risk factors, including suicidal ideation, which is critical in managing psychiatric emergencies. The importance of medication adherence and substance use history was also highlighted in the case.

cases.title: Unresponsive
cases.case_summary: The patient was brought in for an overdose of diazepam. The case was complicated by the patient's history of substance use disorder and the need for careful monitoring. The patient was stabilized and treated appropriately, but the case highlights the importance of thorough assessment and monitoring in overdose situations.

cases.title: Rash

correct_orders.order: Antibiotic: Benzathine penicillin G, 2.4 million units IM

correctly_avoided.order: Laboratory: CT scan of the abdomen/pelvis

cases.case_summary: A 25-year-old male presented with a rash on his palms and soles with a history of sexual encounters that were not protected. The patient was diagnosed with secondary syphilis based on the clinical findings and serological tests. Syphilis is a sexually transmitted infection that can present with a variety of symptoms, including rashes, and it is important to obtain a thorough sexual history to guide diagnosis and treatment. The patient was treated with Benzathine penicillin G, and follow-up was arranged to ensure resolution of symptoms.

cases.title: Shortness of Breath

correctly_avoided.order: Heparin, low molecular weight / Heparin therapy / Heparin sodium / Concomitant / Spinal cord / Aspirin

cases.case_summary:
Differentiate: CHF, pneumonia, embolism, aspiration, heart failure, pneumonia, systemic lupus erythematosus.

The patient has fat embolism syndrome (FES). This typically occurs from hours to days after an initial injury that allows globules (typically from bone marrow) to enter the blood stream. Long bones such as a femur are common causes of FES. FES can also cause respiratory distress, and information on risk factors should be taken, including the patient's history.

The patient has many metabolic abnormalities; the lab results and thrombocytopenia which are very common in fat embolism syndrome. He also has metabolic acidosis, increased level of BUN, and increased creatinine which are also commonly seen in fat embolism syndrome.

Diagnosis is mainly clinical as imaging is not very reliable. Chest x-ray and CT scans can be misleading in this case. The patient could have an abnormal CT scan, but this will take too much time to administer. The patient will require immediate supportive treatment.

A commonly used criteria is the Gurd and Wilson criteria for fat embolism to help clinicians determine if the patient is suffering from a fat embolism. The major criteria are:

1. Pyrexia > 38.0 °C
2. Central nervous system depression
3. Petechiae

There are other criteria, but these will not be listed. The patient, positive in the minor criteria, has tachycardia more than 100 beats per minute, and his globules in plasma in the urine. At least two positive major criteria plus one minor criteria is suggestive of fat embolism. In this case, the patient has safety problems, hypotension, and tachycardia; all of these in turn is a concern order on the EMS.

Treatment is supportive. The patient should be transferred to the ICU, intubated, put on a ventilator. The patient should be given fluids. Albumin therapy is considered experimental and corticosteroids administration is controversial, so the majority treatment is just supportive. One thing to avoid is heparin/anticoagulant treatment. This has shown benefit in fat embolism cases, and it increases the risk of hemorrhage.

cases.title: Found Unconscious
cases.case_summary: This case illustrates the importance of timely evaluation and management of patients who present with altered mental status. The patient was found unconscious and required immediate stabilization and assessment. It is crucial to consider potential causes of altered mental status, including substance use, trauma, and medical conditions. In this case, appropriate imaging and laboratory studies were essential to guide further management.

cases.title: Drowning

correct_orders.order: Basic metabolic profile / Comprehensive metabolic profile / Liver function panel
Saline solution, normal / Lactated Ringer's solution

should_have_ordered.order: Electrocardiography, 12 lead / Electrocardiography, ambulatory / Electrocardiography, rhythm strip, long

correctly_avoided.order: Cardiopulmonary resuscitation, basic / CPR, compressions & breathing

cases.case_summary: Hypothermia patients should be treated immediately before physical exam should be done. The patient has a weak pulse, and CPR should be continued. PALS recommends CPR in patients with a heart rate less than 60 beats per minute who do not respond to positive pressure ventilation. The patient does have a weak pulse. There is a risk of not receiving sufficient blood flow to the brain while performing CPR. The patient should be processed by your hands and not with the machine. The patient should be monitored, and a warming blanket should be given when the patient is hypothermic. Hypothermic patients are almost always confused about their condition. The patient should then be given epinephrine based on the recommendation of Pediatric Advanced Life Support (PALS) protocol.

cases.title: Agitation - Psychiatry

correct_orders.order: Basic metabolic profile / Comprehensive metabolic profile

should_have_ordered.order: Electrocardiography, 12 lead / Electrocardiography, ambulatory / Electrocardiography, rhythm strip, long

cases.case_summary:
Differential diagnosis: Substance induced psychotic disorder, Schizophrenia, Psychosis secondary to a medical condition, Delirium, Epileptic seizures, Electrolyte disturbance

This case is similar to the schizophrenia cases, but this case focuses just on correctly treating acute agitation which is a common occurrence in most emergency departments. As the patient has already assaulted a staff member, IV medication is needed for rapid medication onset to be effective. A typical regimen is colloquially called the 5-2-1 regimen 5 mg of Halidol (Haloperidol), 2 mg of IM Ativan (Lorazepam), and 1 mg of IM Cogentin. As Cogentin is key here, an alternative is 2.5 mg of Halidol, 2 mg of IM Ativan (Lorazepam), and 5 mg of IM Benadryl (Diphenhydramine). Droperidol has its limitations as it is not the best choice. Once the patient is treated, the vital signs can be completed. There can be medical causes of acute agitation which should be ruled out, but given the age of the patient and his history in the hospital, it is unlikely that a medical diagnosis will be determined in this case.

Other second-generation antipsychotics are available for the distressed patient including: IM Olanzapine (Zyprexa), IM Aripiprazole (Abilify), or IM Ziprasidone (Geodon). Halidol is the most commonly administered in hospital emergency settings due to the availability of IM administration along with Lorazepam (Ativan) along with Cogentin or Benadryl to avoid a potential EPS (Extrapyramidal Syndrome) and dyskinesias. This is an area which is being studied as newer antipsychotics will treat agitation as well as psychosis.

cases.title: Muscle Pain

correct_orders.order: Basic metabolic profile / Liver function panel
Saline solution: CAVS / Lactated Ringer solution / Saline solution, normal

should_have_ordered.order: Creatine phosphokinase levels/serum / Creatine phosphokinase, serum, total

cases.case_summary: Rhabdomyolysis is a condition characterized by the breakdown of muscle tissue, leading to the release of muscle fiber contents into the bloodstream. This can result in serious complications, including acute kidney injury. The management of rhabdomyolysis involves identifying the underlying cause, providing supportive care, and monitoring for complications. It is important to maintain adequate hydration and to monitor renal function closely. In some cases, additional treatments such as bicarbonate therapy may be indicated to prevent kidney injury.

cases.title: Sore Throat

correctly_avoided.order: Penicillin V / Penicillin G procaine / Penicillin G benzathine / Amoxicillin and potassium clavulanate / Cephalexin monohydrate / Cefadroxil / cefuroxime sodium / Ceclor / Cefixime / Azithromycin / therapy

cases.case_summary: Differential: Group A Streptococcal Pharyngitis, Retropharyngeal abscess, Epiglottitis, Lemierre Syndrome, Lateral Pharyngeal Abscess, Other Bacterial pharyngitis. This patient has viral pharyngitis. The exact virus will not be determined in the case as it typically is not determined in real life. The main point of the case is to not over-treat. It is important to rule out a Group A Streptococcal infection by doing a rapid group A strep test. If negative, give only symptomatic relief with pain medications (ibuprofen or Acetaminophen) along with salt water gargles which has been shown to help. No other interventions are necessary in this case. In time, the patient will begin to feel better. If you give antibiotics, you would fail the case. Antibiotics can cause harm in viral infections do not help and have led to antibiotic resistance.

cases.title: Sore Throat

should_have_ordered.order: Streptococcal culture, throat, rapid / Streptococcal culture, throat / Bacterial culture, throat / Antibody, streptolysin O, serum / Streptozymes, serum / Bacterial culture, nose

Penicillin V / Penicillin G procaine / penicillin G benzathine / Amoxicillin and potassium clavulanate / Cephalexin monohydrate / Cefadroxil / Cefuroxime sodium / Cefdinir / Ceftazidime / Clarithromycin / Clindamycin / Amoxicillin, therapy / Penicillin G

cases.case_summary:
Differential: Group C and G Streptococcal Pharyngitis, Retropharyngeal Abscess, Epiglottitis, Infectious mononucleosis, Viral Pharyngitis.

Group A Streptococcus (GAS) is the most common cause of bacterial pharyngitis in children. The physical exam and history are consistent with a bacterial pharyngitis. The patient does not have any real symptoms such as congestion or cough. The patient also has a characteristic purplish rash along with tonsillar edema. A rapid strep test should be ordered. It will take too long to make the right decision regarding whether to administer antibiotics or not if the 24 hour strep test is done.

Penicillin/Amoxicillin are the treatment of choice for GAS. Alternatives include Cephalosporins (Cephalexin, Ceftriaxone, Cefuroxime, Cefdinir, Meropenem, Azithromycin or Clindamycin).

Acetaminophen or Tylenol can be administered to treat pain and fever. Once antibiotics are given, the patient will feel better. The patient should be counseled to take the entire regimen of antibiotics to avoid developing antibiotic resistance.

cases.title: Painful Rash - Pediatrics

correct_orders.order: Basic metabolic profile / Comprehensive metabolic profile

should_have_ordered.order: Bacterial culture, wound / bacterial culture, skin

cases.case_summary: Differentiate systemic symptoms from Stevens-Johnson syndrome versus other illness elements. The patient should be monitored closely for signs of infection. The patient should be admitted to the ICU and the treatment should include the use of supportive care and possible immunosuppressive therapy.

cases.title: Leg Pain - Internal Medicine

correct_orders.order: PT/PTT / Fibrin thromboplastin time AND PT/INR
Doppler: lower extremities, venous / Duplex ultrasonography, lower extremities, venous

should_have_ordered.order: Basic metabolic profile / Comprehensive metabolic profile

cases.case_summary:
The patient has classical symptoms of DVT. The risk factors for DVT include: obesity, tenderness at area of interest, pitting edema, calf swelling 3cm larger than the other leg, and distal swelling of the leg. Use of the Wells Score.

The D-Dimer results will be elevated in DVT, but this would be a high probability due to risk factors including a positive PVT. A calf ultrasound (duplex) should be done to confirm the diagnosis. The patient should be put on anticoagulants. The options for anticoagulants for DVT are: LMWH heparin (Lovenox) / Warfarin (Coumadin) CR Lovenox / dalteparin CR Lovenox / edoxaban OR Rivaroxaban monotherapy OR Apixaban Monotherapy.

cases.title: Chest Pain
cases.case_summary: This case illustrates that patients with chest pain can have a variety of presentations, and it is important to recognize the potential for serious conditions such as myocardial infarction. The importance of timely intervention cannot be overstated, as delays can lead to significant morbidity and mortality. In this case, the patient presented with silent ischemia, which can be particularly challenging to diagnose. It is crucial to maintain a high index of suspicion and to utilize appropriate diagnostic tools to ensure timely treatment.

cases.title: Shortness of Breath

You were in the wrong location (Emergency Department) at simulated time 0 Days, 04 Hours, 10 Minutes. Correct location at this time was: Intensive Care Unit or Inpatient Unit. The patient did not receive appropriate treatment in the correct environment.

cases.title: Rash - Pediatrics

correct_orders.order: Basic metabolic profile / Comprehensive metabolic profile

should_have_ordered.order: Immune globulin / Rho (D) immune globulin / Dexamethasone / Prednisone / Methylprednisolone / Prednisolone

correctly_avoided.order: Celecoxib / Diclofenac sodium / Etodolac / Fenoprofen / Ibuprofen / Indomethacin / Ketoprofen / Ketorolac tromethamine / Meclofenamate sodium / Mefenamic acid / Nabumetone / Naproxen / Choline / Aspirin / Salicylate / Tetanus / therapy / Aspirin, therapy

cases.case_summary:
This patient in this case has immune thrombocytopenia (ITP). The patient most likely developed ITP after having a recent viral infection. ITP patients will have platelet counts less than 100,000/microl, with a normal white blood cell count and hemoglobin. The majority of children develop ITP after a recent viral illness. The patient has gingival bleeding and petechiae. This would classify the patient as a grade I/IV between moderate and severe. The patient will require treatment with either glucocorticoids, IVIG or both. 

The patient at risk for potentially severe bleeding if injured, so the patient should be counseled to avoid contact sports as well as to avoid antiplatelet medications like Aspirin and NSAIDs. Depending on the treatment choice, the patient can be treated either inpatient or outpatient. Once the patient gets treatment, he will improve.

cases.title: Cough
cases.case_summary: Legionella pneumonia is a type of atypical pneumonia that is caused by the bacterium Legionella pneumophila. It is important to recognize this condition as it can lead to severe illness and requires specific treatment.

cases.title: Back Pain

correct_orders.order: Basic metabolic profile / Comprehensive metabolic profile
Creatine phosphokinase / Serum / Sedimentation rate, erythrocyte

correctly_avoided.order: CT: abdomen/pelvis, with contrast / CT: abdomen/pelvis, without contrast
MRI: abdomen/pelvis, with gadolinium / MRI: abdomen/pelvis, without gadolinium

cases.case_summary:
Differential: Chronic Nonspecific Back Pain, Fibromyalgia, Vertebral Compression Fracture, Sacroiliac joint infection, Diffuse Idiopathic Skeletal Hyperostosis.

The patient has Ankylosing Spondylitis. The patient has classic symptoms of Ankylosing Spondylitis which include back pain, morning stiffness, and a history of psoriasis. These increase the likelihood of having Spondylitis.

Initial evaluation should be a physical exam focusing on extremities and eyes for iritis/uveitis. Other tests include HLA-B27, and X-rays of the pelvis and spine.

Once the diagnosis of Ankylosing Spondylitis is made, treatment should be with a TNF inhibitors Etanercept, Infliximab, Adalimumab (not an order on the USMLE), but TNF inhibitors in 1 inhibitors are not yet available as orders on the USMLE which include Secukinumab and Ixekizumab. Interleukin 17 inhibitors are also treatment options for Ankylosing Spondylitis.

Once the correct treatment is given, the patient will begin to feel better after a week, and the case will end.

cases.title: Scrotal Pain

correct_orders.order: Antibiotics, direct fluorescent, chlamydia, urethra / Chlamydia trachomatis DNA probe test, urine / Chlamydia trachomatis DNA probe test, urethra / Antigen, bacterial, chlamydia, urine / Chlamydia trachomatis culture, urethra
Neisseria gonorrhoeae DNA probe test, urethra / Neisseria gonorrhoeae DNA probe test, urine / Antigen, bacterial, gonococcus, urine / Bacterial antigens detection, gonococcus, urine
Basic metabolic profile / Comprehensive metabolic profile

Acetaminophen / Gentamicin, therapy / Ceftriaxone sodium AND Ciprofloxacin / Ceftriaxone sodium AND Gentamicin / Ceftriaxone sodium AND Levofloxacin / Ceftriaxone sodium AND Moxifloxacin hydrochloride / Ceftriaxone sodium AND Metronidazole / Ceftriaxone sodium AND Doxycycline
Ice applied to the scrotum can help decrease swelling and pain.

cases.case_summary:
Differential: Testicular Torsion, Trauma, Inguinal Hernia, Testicular cancer, Referred pain.

The patient has Acute Epididymitis. Due to the age of the patient and his risk sexual partners, his acute Acute Epididymitis is most likely from an infectious source. Imaging is not required in this case as the Physical exam shows tenderness and swelling of the epididymis. The patient’s clinical picture reflects the quality would be negative in testicular torsion.

The most common infectious causes of Acute Epididymitis are N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis. These should be tested for, but they are commonly negative in those who do not have urinary tract symptoms such as in this case. As the patient practices safe sex, co-infection between the two could be the cause of his Acute Epididymitis.

This case can be treated as an outpatient. The patient can be treated with oral antibiotics, NSAIDS, application of ice to the scrotum, and scrotal elevation.

The antibiotic choice in this case would be ceftriaxone plus a fluoroquinolone to cover all the possible culprits of gonorrhea, chlamydia, and rectal flora.

After 48 hours of treatment, the patient will begin to feel better, and the case will end. The patient should be counseled on safe sex practices.

cases.title: Muscle Weakness
cases.diagnosis: Hyperkalemic Emergency Secondary to ACE Inhibitor and NSAID Use

correct_orders.order: Basic metabolic profile / Electrolytes, serum / Comprehensive metabolic profile

should_have_ordered.order: Albuterol / Insulin, regular AND Dextrose: 10% in normal saline solution / Insulin, regular AND Dextrose: 10% in water / Insulin, regular AND Dextrose: 25% in normal saline solution / Insulin, regular AND Dextrose: 5% in normal saline solution / Insulin, regular AND Dextrose: 50% in water

cases.case_summary: Calcium chloride, insulin, hyperkalemia, potassium predisposition. Patients with hyperkalemic emergencies due to NSAID and ACE inhibitor use should be treated with calcium, insulin, and dextrose. These patients will exhibit signs of hyperkalemia. Cardiac status is very important in these patients. Dialysis can be attempted first, and if the patient continues to be refractory from initial treatment, then dialysis can be considered.

cases.title: Shortness of Breath
cases.case_summary: The patient has sarcoidosis. Sarcoidosis can present with respiratory symptoms and is often diagnosed by imaging studies. Research on the clinical management of sarcoidosis suggests that initial treatment is typically corticosteroids, and this is primarily for those with significant respiratory symptoms. The results from imaging studies should be reviewed, as they can provide information on the extent of the disease. The case summary highlights the importance of considering sarcoidosis in patients presenting with respiratory symptoms and the need for appropriate diagnostic studies to confirm the diagnosis.

cases.title: Wrist and Ankle Pain
cases.case_summary: This case illustrates the importance of considering the differential diagnosis for joint pain in a patient with known rheumatoid arthritis. The clinician should be aware of the potential for complications related to the disease and the need for appropriate imaging and laboratory studies to guide management.

cases.title: Fatigue
cases.case_summary:
Differential: Acute Kidney Disease, Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease (ADPKD), Medullary Cystic Kidney Disease, IgA Nephropathy, Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis, Fabry disease.

The patient has end stage renal failure likely from diabetes mellitus and hypertension. The patient is not active with a creatinine of 9.4. The patient has primary metabolic acidosis with an increased anion gap with full exploratory compensation. The patient has too many metabolic abnormalities to be treated as an outpatient (including a possibility of 6), the patient will require inpatient hospitalization and dialysis.

Nephrology should be consulted to help with the management of the patient. A kidney transplant will be needed as a long-term solution.

Indications for dialysis include needing nutritional status, fatigue, muscle mass, and cognitive impairment, hyperkalemia, refractory acidosis. The patient in this case easily meets these criteria.

Once the patient is put on dialysis, the patient will begin to feel better, and the case will end.

We have received emails regarding why this patient requires hemodialysis. The patient is symptomatic and has a creatinine of 9.4. The calculated eGFR based on a 60-year-old would be mL/min/1.73m2. The patient is having memory problems. Your doctor, and is very well right; the patient will require dialysis. Dialysis indications are the following - eGFR is < 15 mL/min/1.73m2, uremic encephalopathy (the patient has been having episodes of confusion which is likely due to uremic encephalopathy), cognitive impairment, hyperkalemia, fatigue and malaise, persistent volume overload, and declining nutritional status. The patient clearly meets these criteria. Efforts to treat the hyperkalemia with various medications prior to hemodialysis are not needed as hemodialysis will treat the hyperkalemia and other metabolic problems.

cases.title: Lip Lesion
cases.diagnosis: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Lip

correct_orders.order: Biopsy, oral lesion (cavity) / Biopsy, skin / Biopsy, skin lesion, excisional
Basic metabolic profile / Comprehensive metabolic profile

correctly_avoided.order: No inappropriate invasive orders were administer to the patient during this case.

cases.case_summary: The patient has squamous cell carcinoma of the lip. This is a fairly straight forward case. You diagnose by a biopsy and then remove the tumor by consulting dermatology to perform Mohs surgery. Mohs surgery tends to be the gold standard for removing facial tumors as it minimizes tissue loss. The Mohs surgeon will remove a layer where the tumor was located and take the tissue, dye it, and look at the histology of the removed tissue. If there are still cancer cells in the specimen, the Mohs surgeon will go back and remove more of the lesion and then there is no more cancer. The patient will wait in the office while the Mohs surgeon looks at the tissue, so that the surgeon can remove more tissue if necessary. Mohs surgery will preserve the most tissue and have the best cosmetic outcome which is important to most patients. Mohs surgery is an outpatient procedure and can be done with local anesthetic. Once the surgery is completed, the case will end.

cases.title: Headache
cases.case_summary: The patient is a 30-year-old female with a history of headaches and visual disturbances. She presents with worsening headaches and transient visual obscurations. The diagnosis of idiopathic intracranial hypertension is made based on clinical findings and imaging studies. Treatment options include weight loss and medications to reduce intracranial pressure. A referral to ophthalmology is also warranted for further evaluation of her visual symptoms.

cases.title: Seizures - Pediatrics

should_have_ordered.order: Basic metabolic profile / Comprehensive metabolic profile

cases.case_summary:
Differential: Beta-ketothiolase deficiency, Glycine encephalopathy, Methylmalonic acidemia, Propionic acidemia.

The patient has the signs of classic Maple Syrup Urine Disease (MSUD) which include irritability, poor feeding, bicycling movements, focal dystonia, and seizures. The are 5 types of MSUD (Classic, Intermediate, Intermittent, Thiamine-responsive, and E3-deficient). Classic MSUD is the most common. The amino acid levels are given in two because these orders are not present on the USMLE at this time. There are no orders for serum or urine levels of leucine, valine, isoleucine, or leucine. However, you must put together that high amino acid levels with the presentation to come up with the MSUD diagnosis.

The treatment for MSUD is a no-protein/low-protein diet. This specialized diet would be life-long much like PKU. The patient would not be able to breast-feed and would need a non-branched-chain amino acids (meaning low, leucine, valine, isoleucine) formula. There is no specific diet for non-branched-chain amino acid diet, so a low-protein/protein diet is utilized. It is recommended that all patients with MSUD be treated with a thiamine to see if the patient has the Thiamine-responsive type. If there is no improvement after 4 weeks, then the thiamine treatment can be discontinued.

A potential long term treatment of MSUD is liver transplantation. As the patient is only a week old, this discussion would happen at a later time and would depend on the outcome of diet modification.

Note that in this case, the CBC/ CMP will be normal which is not abnormal as the patient seems to be suffering more neurological damage than metabolic issues. However, metabolic decompensation is a possibility, so the patient should be followed closely. Admitting to the hospital/ICU is appropriate until the patient can be stabilized and amino acid levels returned to normal levels. MSUD is more severe than PKU as this can lead to death.

cases.title: Poor Feeding

correct_orders.order: Labs: Comprehensive metabolic profile / Liver function panel

cases.case_summary: This is a difficult case that may require a multi-faceted feeding approach to resolve. It is tempting to just put the baby under bright lights and advance the clock, however, there are concerning findings which include jaundice and poor feeding, which would be red flags for HEM and can lead to severe issues if not addressed promptly. The infant would benefit from closer monitoring and possible genetic evaluation.

cases.title: Chronic Diarrhea
cases.case_summary: A patient presents with chest pain and is diagnosed with Acute Myocardial Ischemia (AMI). The review page shows that the healthcare provider should have ordered aspirin, atenolol, clopidogrel, and tirofiban to manage the condition. Magnesium sulfate was inappropriately ordered, and nitroglycerine should be avoided.

correctly_ordered.rationale: It's standard first-line therapy for AMI
It's a beta blocker and can help reduce myocardial ischemia risk

should_have_ordered.rationale: It's an antiplatelet medication used to prevent arterial thrombosis
It's a gpIIa inhibitor that can help reduce the risk of thromboembolic events

correctly_avoided.rationale: It's not indicated for AMI as it does not have proven benefits in terms of clinical outcomes

inappropriate_orders.reason: It can increase the risk of myocardial ischemia due to its vasodilatory effects

cases.title: Abnormal Behavior
cases.case_summary: This case involves a patient who was administered Hepatitis B before Hepatitis A. While the first dose is important for immunity, the sequence of vaccinations should ideally be followed to ensure maximum protection against hepatitis and other related infections.

correctly_ordered.rationale: Vaccination schedule typically follows an age-based order of administration
Hepatitis B often follows Hepatitis A vaccination

should_have_ordered.rationale: Should be administered after Hepatitis B for a more comprehensive vaccination series

correctly_avoided.rationale: Vaccinations should not be administered out of order

inappropriate_orders.order: Hepatitis B, Hepatitis C, Hepatitis A
inappropriate_orders.reason: Sequencing of vaccines should follow the recommended schedule
